memos.markdown:
# The AI Capex Bill Comes Due — and the Market Started Reading It

The tape this week posed one question in three different fonts: is the AI buildout worth what it costs? **Apple briefly overtook Nvidia as the world's most valuable company** — not because Apple did anything, but because investors punished the companies spending hardest on AI compute. **Micron printed a record $41.5 billion quarter and got a ~25% rout** for its trouble. **SK Hynix sits 38% off its highs.** When the memory names selling into the boom trade like the boom is over, the market is telling you it no longer accepts capex as its own reward.

## Deal activity

The week's headline deal wasn't a sale — it was a lease. **Meta and Anthropic are in talks for a compute arrangement worth up to $10 billion**, and the framing matters: a leading AI lab renting a hyperscaler's silicon ahead of its own IPO. That's not a merger; it's Anthropic pre-funding its infrastructure obligations off someone else's balance sheet, and Meta discovering it may have a new revenue line to justify the capex the market is currently skeptical of. Two problems, one contract.

Everything else was a strategic tuck-in parade. The week produced roughly eight closed acquisitions with not a single disclosed value among them — **NINJIO/SafeStack** in security training and **CSL Group/IoTM Solutions** in eSIM orchestration are the cleanest examples of the pattern: software roll-ups buying capability, not scale. The undisclosed values are the tell. This is portfolio gardening, not conviction dealmaking. Two live threads carry actual numbers and both are stuck: **Blackstone extended its TXNM Energy merger** deadline rather than close it, and the **~$580M Nextflex** auction in Korea slipped its final-bid deadline with no buyers named. Delay is the theme; nobody wants to be the one who overpaid at the top.

## Capital markets

The IPO window is cracked open and letting in mostly retail air. **BOXABL completed its de-SPAC with FG Merger II at a stated $3.5 billion valuation** — "aspirational" is the filing's word, not ours — with trading set to begin July 20. **FreeCast filed its S-1** and **3KNIGHTS DYNAMICS amended its F-1**, both small, both unpriced. The quality bar here is not high.

On the debt side, the calendar was routine to the point of quiet: **JCP&L, a FirstEnergy subsidiary, launched an exchange offer for up to $350M of 4.600% notes due 2030.** Housekeeping, not a signal. The real capital-markets story is the equity repricing underneath — the Nasdaq fell 1.40% on a semiconductor selloff, and the memory complex led the way down.

## What we're watching

First, **Alphabet earnings and its capex guidance**, which the sector is treating as the referendum. After a week of watching Micron and Meta get marked down for spending, GOOGL's number will either validate the ROI panic or break it.

Second, the **Meta–Anthropic compute deal's structure**. Whether it lands as a lease, a prepay, or something with equity attached will tell you how a pre-IPO Anthropic intends to finance itself — and whether hyperscalers can turn AI capex into billable revenue.

Third, **BOXABL's Nasdaq debut on July 20** as a live read on de-SPAC appetite in a jittery tape.

Bottom line: the market has stopped paying for AI capex on faith, and every name from Micron to Meta is now being asked to show the return.

# The Chip Reset Meets the AI Infrastructure Bill

Two numbers define the week, and they point in opposite directions. Semiconductor stocks logged their **worst month since 2008**, with Intel off 3%, SK Hynix at a fresh 12-month low, and Nvidia's crown suddenly contestable. Yet the capital committed to AI buildout kept climbing: TSMC confirmed a **$100 billion** Phoenix expansion and Meta greenlit a **$50 billion** Louisiana data center. The tape says chips are broken; the checkbooks say the opposite. Add Apple retaking the market-cap throne from Nvidia for the first time since April 2025, and the week's real story is a rotation—away from picks-and-shovels multiples, toward the balance sheets funding the dig.

## Deal activity

The private market spoke in one very loud voice: **$19 billion**. TeraWulf's reported deal with Anthropic—featuring a $3.5B upfront payment already drawing scrutiny over financing adequacy—is the print that matters, and it tells you where compute demand is being underwritten. Everything else was smaller and structural. Spark AI Foundry's non-binding bid for EdgeMode's ready-to-build 300 MW Spanish project and Micron's **$500M** Texas wafer arrangement round out the theme: capacity, wherever you can secure it.

Below the marquee names, the week was a roll-up grind. PartnerOne, ORTEC, VisualLogix, and a handful of others closed undisclosed software tuck-ins—the kind of deals that keep enterprise consolidation humming without moving a needle. The tell worth flagging: Apple is reported to be acquiring **SigScalr**, an AI infrastructure asset, which fits neatly with an analyst thesis that Apple's "wait and see" restraint is now a competitive advantage. Two closings stalled—Korea's Nextflex ($580M) saw its final-bid process delayed, and the Skydance/Paramount merger drew an antitrust challenge from state AGs. Deals are closing; the friction is regulatory and structural, not appetite.

## Capital markets

Credit is doing the heavy lifting the equity market won't. NBIS, the Nvidia-backed builder, landed a **$775M debt deal** for AI infrastructure and rallied on it—private credit remains wide open for data-center paper. The asset-backed market echoed the point: AIP Capital and Monroe closed a **$643M** inaugural aircraft ABS, a reminder that structured appetite is broad, not just AI-adjacent. CreditSights' mid-year hyperscaler update frames the backdrop—issuance and capex are climbing in tandem, and someone has to fund the generators (Amazon alone is deploying 280 of them at one Pennsylvania site).

New equity issuance was thin. Advasa Holdings filed an S-1/A under ticker ADBT with no proceeds disclosed, and Zepto's IPO valuation is reportedly being cut sharply ahead of listing. When a growth-market name trims before it prints, that's the window telling you it's narrower than the headlines suggest.

## What we're watching

First, earnings. Alphabet and Tesla headline the week, and Alphabet is the one to size—the stock whipsawed on Gemini-delay concerns even as cloud and ad growth held valuation. A capacity-constrained Google guiding capex materially higher would confirm the infrastructure thesis in real time.

Second, the TeraWulf/Anthropic structure. That $3.5B upfront question isn't academic; how it's financed sets the template for the next wave of compute-supply deals.

Third, the regulatory calendar around Skydance/Paramount. The AG challenge introduces genuine timing risk to a deal the market had penciled as done—watch for a response that either clears the path or extends it.

Bottom line: the semiconductor selloff is a valuation reset, not a demand reset—and the week's $170B-plus in committed capex and credit is the receipt.

# The Chip Reset Meets the Infrastructure Bill Coming Due

The tape delivered two stories this week that don't rhyme. Chip equities logged **their worst month since 2008**, dragging Intel down 3%, SK Hynix to a fresh 12-month low, and the whole complex into what one analyst gamely called a "reset." Yet the money committed to AI infrastructure kept swelling: TSMC put another **$100 billion** into Phoenix, Meta greenlit a **$50 billion** Hyperion campus in Louisiana, and Anthropic and TeraWulf signed a **$19 billion** data-center pact. The reset is happening in public markets; the buildout is happening on balance sheets. And the balance sheets are winning the argument for now.

## Deal activity

Strategic tuck-ins, not headline M&A. The week's closed transactions were a parade of roll-ups and capability grabs with no disclosed values — PartnerOne bolting on ISI Analytics, CSL Group absorbing an eSIM platform, ORTEC buying a Belgian workforce provider serving ~225,000 frontline workers. Useful, unglamorous, and telling: buyers are shopping for tools, not scale.

The names that matter are the pending ones. **Anthropic's $19 billion** arrangement with TeraWulf — a **$3.5 billion** upfront slug — is already drawing scrutiny over whether the financing actually supports the commitment, which is the right question to ask when a frontier lab underwrites a miner's capex. Apple's rumored purchase of data-AI shop **SigScalr** fits its "wait and see" AI posture: buy the plumbing, skip the arms race. Micron's **$500 million** Texas wafer arrangement extends the domestic-supply theme; Spark AI's non-binding bid for a 300 MW ready-to-build site in Spain shows compute real estate is now an asset class of its own.

Two deals are stuck. Korea's Nextflex sale (~**$580 million**) slipped its final-bid deadline with no buyer in sight, and state AGs filed an antitrust challenge to **Skydance-Paramount**. When a flexible-circuit auction and a media merger both stall the same week, the read is financing discipline plus regulatory friction — not a market that's closed.

## Capital markets

Credit is carrying the load equities won't. NBIS, the Nvidia-backed builder, landed a **$775 million** debt package to fund its buildout — a clean signal that private credit's appetite for AI data centers hasn't cooled even as chip stocks correct. AIP Capital and Monroe closed an inaugural **$643 million** aircraft ABS, evidence that asset-based finance remains open for the right collateral. CreditSights' mid-year hyperscaler note frames the backdrop: issuance and capex are both climbing, and the bond market is happily funding the trench-digging.

The equity new-issue window is another story. Advasa Holdings filed an S-1/A (ticker ADBT) with no proceeds disclosed, and Zepto is reportedly cutting its IPO valuation "sharply" ahead of listing. Emerging-market tech appetite is thin; US software is testing, not committing. Meanwhile Sherritt noteholders floated a rival recapitalization plan — a reminder that restructuring desks are staying warm.

## What we're watching

Alphabet and Tesla headline earnings, and Alphabet is the one to parse: **8 news mentions** this week versus a baseline near zero, a 4% Friday drop, and a Gemini delay set against capacity constraints that could push capex higher. The print sets large-cap AI sentiment.

Skydance-Paramount's antitrust timeline is the near-term regulatory tell for media consolidation — watch whether the AGs' challenge forces a remedy or just delay.

Finally, the TeraWulf-Anthropic financing structure. If a **$3.5 billion** upfront can't be cleanly funded, it reprices how the market underwrites lab-to-infrastructure commitments across the board.

**Bottom line:** the chips corrected, but the checks kept clearing — and this cycle is now a credit story wearing a semiconductor costume.

# The Week the IPO Queue Became the Story

Three filings now sit in registration targeting a combined ~$3.5 trillion — SpaceX ($1.75T), Anthropic (~$965B), and OpenAI (~$852B) — an equity-supply test with no historical precedent. Against that backdrop, the week's announced M&A was modest in count but pointed in direction: chipmakers buying software.

## Deal activity

Qualcomm's ~$4B agreement for Modular is the week's signature transaction — the third case this cycle of silicon vendors acquiring the compiler/software layer rather than more silicon, aimed squarely at loosening the CUDA lock-in. Adobe's purchase of Topaz Labs (terms undisclosed) extends its pattern of buying model-adjacent imaging capability. Google/Wiz ($32B) remains in regulatory review with closing targeted for later this year.

## Capital markets

The convert market continues to reopen for AI infrastructure names: CoreWeave's 1.75% notes due 2031 traded above par this week, a meaningful signal after the winter selloff in neocloud credit. Quantinuum's June debut ($1.68B raised) continues to trade well, keeping the quantum window open. HBM supply reportedly sold out through 2027 shifts pricing power — and, eventually, capex financing needs — toward the memory complex.

## What we're watching

SpaceX float-size decisions, which will set allocation dynamics for the entire queue behind it; the Wiz closing timeline as the year's largest pending strategic deal; and TSMC's monthly sales print as the cleanest near-term read on AI accelerator demand.

Bottom line: supply of paper is about to test demand for the AI trade — and the acquirers who move before that repricing settle their currency questions first.

*(Example memo compiled from seed data as of Jul 6, 2026 — live memos begin with the first Friday run.)*
